serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of dried basil is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 1.4 g), used as a seasoning in sauces, soups, and pasta dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Spices, basil, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 02003)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried basil (Ocimum basilicum) used as primary nutrient reference. Input HPB values are consistent with USDA data and are retained where aligned. USDA values used for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and additional micronutrients not present in HPB source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Herbs and Spices — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database low-GI herb category; dried basil has negligible available carbohydrate and is classified low-GI by analogy with other dried culinary herbs
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dried basil is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 5 (low) by analogy with other dried culinary herbs with negligible available carbohydrate content. Available carbohydrate per 100g = 47.75g total carbohydrate minus 37.7g dietary fibre = approximately 10.05g available carbohydrate. GL per 1.4g serving = (5 × 0.14g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.03. This is a conservative analogous estimate; the food is used in gram-level quantities and has negligible glycaemic impact in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (76 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (2630 mg/100g) with high dietary fibre (37.7 g/100g), supporting DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Dried basil is highly suitable for hypertensive patients; its exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio (>34:1) and high fibre content actively support blood pressure reduction when used regularly as a culinary herb, though per-serving quantities (0.5–2 g) mean cumulative use across meals is needed for meaningful nutrient contribution.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low sugar (1.71 g/100g), very high dietary fibre (37.7 g/100g), and bioactive compounds (eugenol, rosmarinic acid) with evidence-based hypoglycaemic and insulin-sensitising properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried basil is appropriate for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible sugar content, high fibre, and low glycaemic index (estimated GI ~5) make it a safe seasoning, and its polyphenolic compounds may provide additive hypoglycaemic benefit with regular culinary use, though patients on antidiabetic medication should be aware of potential cumulative hypoglycaemic effects.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (2.16 g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, high potassium (2630 mg/100g), high dietary fibre (37.7 g/100g), and anti-inflammatory polyphenols (eugenol, rosmarinic acid, orientin)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried basil is cardioprotective as a culinary herb; its low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and anti-inflammatory phytochemicals support cardiovascular health, and regular inclusion in a herb-rich diet is consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, eugenol, orientin), very high calcium (2240 mg/100g) for bone protection, and high fibre supporting gut immune modulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried basil is suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; its anti-inflammatory polyphenols and high calcium content support joint and bone health, and regular use as part of a herb-rich diet may contribute to cumulative anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried basil (Ocimum basilicum) is the dehydrated form of sweet basil leaves, presenting as dark green flakes with a concentrated sweet, aromatic, and slightly peppery flavour profile. It is produced by air-drying or low-temperature dehydration of fresh basil leaves, which concentrates its nutrients and volatile oils. Widely used in Mediterranean, Italian, and global cuisines, dried basil is a staple seasoning in pasta sauces, pizza, soups, stews, salads, and marinades. Nutritionally, dried basil is exceptionally dense per 100g: it provides 233 kcal, 22.98g protein, 4.07g fat, 47.75g carbohydrate, and a remarkable 37.7g dietary fibre. Its mineral profile is outstanding — calcium at 2240 mg/100g, potassium at 2630 mg/100g, and iron at 89.8 mg/100g — though these values reflect the concentrated dry weight and actual per-serving intake (typically 0.5–2g) is modest. Dried basil contains bioactive polyphenols including rosmarinic acid, eugenol, and orientin, which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, hypoglycaemic, and insulin-sensitising properties in peer-reviewed research. It is suitable for patients with hypertension (very low sodium, high potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, high fibre, hypoglycaemic phytochemicals), cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, anti-inflammatory compounds), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory polyphenols, high calcium). Dried basil is vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is a minimally processed herb with no allergens and is appropriate for use across all dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Is dried basil good for diabetes?
Can I use dried basil if I have high blood pressure?
What are the nutrients in dried basil?
How much dried basil is a serving?
Dried basil vs fresh basil nutrition
Is dried basil vegan and gluten-free?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values from HPB Singapore are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for dried basil (FDC ID 170921). Both approved sources agree closely on macronutrients, fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, and iron. Additional micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate (low category) as dried basil is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which reduces GI confidence to medium for that specific field, but overall record confidence remains high.
notes: 1. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dried basil). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.24g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.44g), saturated_fat_g (2.16g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170921) as HPB source did not report fatty acid breakdown. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (~5, low category) as dried basil is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimate is based on negligible available carbohydrate content and analogy with other dried culinary herbs. GL per 1.4g serving ≈ 0.03. 4. Iron value of 89.8 mg/100g is exceptionally high but is consistent with both HPB and USDA SR Legacy data for dried basil (USDA reports 89.78 mg/100g); this reflects the concentrated dry weight of the herb and is not an error. 5. Calcium value of 2240 mg/100g is similarly high and consistent with USDA SR Legacy (2240 mg/100g); again reflects dry-weight concentration. 6. The input dietary_tag 'wholegrain' has been removed as dried basil is an herb, not a grain product. 7. Occasion tag 'snack' from input has been retained in meal_context as 'seasoning' is more accurate; dried basil is a herb/condiment, not a snack food. 8. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches; FoodEx2 code A00LQ is the closest available match for dried basil in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
